text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Sarah Nguyen
Elm Grove Surgery

14 Elm Grove, Southwark, London SE15 3QP

Dear Dr Sarah Nguyen

Daniel Hart, DOB: 03-05-1987, Hospital No: H739204 NHS No. 8429175603
Flat 12, 4 Baxter Mews, London SE1 5LP

Diagnosis/summary: Recurrent seizures of undetermined classification; prior EEGs inconclusive; MRI brain (2015) reported as normal. Works in a manual/safety‑sensitive environment involving heights and machinery.

Previous anti-seizure medicines tried: lamotrigine (partial benefit but persistent events), levetiracetam (mood irritability), carbamazepine (dizziness), valproate (weight gain, stopped), lacosamide (no clear benefit), clobazam PRN (sedation). Not previously tried: topiramate, zonisamide, perampanel, brivaracetam, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide.

I reviewed Daniel Hart in clinic today. He attended alone. He reports ongoing intermittent episodes characterised by a brief warning of “pressure in the head,” followed by behavioural arrest and confusion for a few minutes, with fatigue afterwards. He works on scaffolding and near powered equipment; his employer is aware and has temporarily adjusted duties pending medical advice.

Event frequency: A short clip recorded by his partner last month aligns with the description above and indicates that these episodes are still occurring several times per week. He notes occasional tongue biting and two nocturnal events over the past six weeks. No clear triggers identified; sleep deprivation may worsen symptoms.

Medications at present:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Folic acid 5 mg daily
- PRN clobazam 10 mg for cluster (uses rarely due to sedation)

Adverse effects: reports mild tremor and reduced concentration late afternoon; otherwise tolerating current regimen.

Examination today: normal general and neurological examination. Weight stable; blood pressure 122/74. No stigmata of systemic illness.

Plan:
- Discussed treatment options including trial of topiramate or zonisamide, and non-pharmacological strategies (sleep regularity, alcohol moderation). Given his safety‑sensitive role, we agreed on cautious titration with close follow‑up.
- Initiate topiramate 25 mg nightly for 1 week, then 25 mg twice daily for 1 week, increasing by 25 mg twice daily each week as tolerated to a target of 100 mg twice daily. Counselled on potential adverse effects: cognitive slowing, weight loss, paraesthesia, mood change, kidney stones; advised good hydration and to report visual symptoms urgently.
- Arrange nurse-led telephone review in 2–3 weeks, then monthly, to monitor tolerability and adjust dose.
- Requested updated MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and prolonged ambulatory EEG to better characterise events.
- Work and safety: advised avoidance of working at height, operating unguarded machinery, and lone working until event control and further assessment are achieved; I have provided a brief occupational health note today to support temporary adjusted duties.
- Driving: standard DVLA guidance discussed; he confirms he is not currently driving.

I will review him in clinic in 3 months or earlier if problems arise. Please continue prescriptions as above and let me know if there are concerns.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: still occurring several times per week.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "these episodes are still occurring several times per week." This is the clearest current frequency. It also mentions "two nocturnal events over the past six weeks" (~0.33 per week), which is less frequent. Per the rule to select the highest frequency when multiple are provided, we use the weekly frequency. Because "several" is non-numeric, we normalize it to the allowed format using 'multiple'. Final label: multiple per week.
seizure_frequency_number: still occurring several times per week.

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
